shortDescription.text: Unauthenticated endpoint allows arbitrary workflow invocation
Webhook ID enumeration allows unauthorized workflow triggering
Internal error details leaked to external callers
Debug endpoint exposes internal URLs and tenant metadata
No rate limiting on unauthenticated webhook endpoints

message.text: The webhook endpoint at /triggers/webhook/<webhook_id> accepts GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, and OPTIONS methods without any authentication or authorization checks. Any external caller who knows or guesses a webhook_id can invoke the associated workflow. The only gate is knowledge of the webhook_id string, which is passed directly to WebhookService.get_webhook_trigger_and_workflow() for a database lookup. There is no HMAC signature verification, no shared secret validation, and no IP allowlisting.

Webhook IDs serve as the sole authorization token for triggering workflows. The webhook_id is a plain string path parameter looked up directly in the database (WorkflowWebhookTrigger.webhook_id == webhook_id). If webhook IDs are predictable (e.g., sequential integers, short UUIDs, or low-entropy tokens), an attacker can enumerate valid IDs and trigger arbitrary tenant workflows. The lack of additional authentication means possession of the ID equals authorization.

The handle_webhook exception handler catches all exceptions and returns the raw exception message to the caller via str(e) in the JSON response body: `jsonify({"error": "Internal server error", "message": str(e)}), 500`. This can leak internal implementation details such as database table names, SQL error messages, file paths, or service configuration to unauthenticated external callers. The debug endpoint (handle_webhook_debug) correctly returns a generic message instead.

The debug webhook endpoint at /triggers/webhook-debug/<webhook_id> is also unauthenticated. When no active debug listener is registered, the 409 response includes the production webhook URL via `webhook_trigger.webhook_url` in the `execution_url` field. This leaks the production trigger URL to any unauthenticated caller who discovers a debug webhook ID, potentially enabling them to invoke the production workflow.

Neither the production webhook endpoint nor the debug webhook endpoint implements rate limiting at the controller or decorator level. While backend AppTriggerStatus.RATE_LIMITED status exists as an account-level quota mechanism, there is no per-IP or per-webhook request rate limiting to prevent brute-force enumeration of webhook IDs or denial-of-service via high-volume webhook invocations that consume workflow execution resources.
